questions.question: What is the primary purpose of CSS Modules?
options: To provide local scoping of class names by default
questions.explanation: CSS Modules automatically scope class names locally by generating unique hashed class names, preventing global naming conflicts.

questions.question: Which file extension indicates a CSS Module file?
questions.explanation: CSS Modules use the .module.css naming convention (e.g., Button.module.css) to tell the bundler to process them as CSS Modules.

questions.question: How do you import a CSS Module in a React component?
questions.explanation: CSS Modules are imported as an object: import styles from './Button.module.css', then used as styles.className.

questions.question: How do you apply a CSS Module class in React JSX?
options: Both B and C are valid
questions.explanation: Both styles.button (dot notation) and styles['button'] (bracket notation) work, though bracket notation is needed for hyphenated class names.

questions.question: What does CSS Modules do with class names at build time?
options: Appends a unique hash to create scoped class names
questions.explanation: CSS Modules appends a deterministic hash to class names (e.g., .button becomes .Button_button_1a2b3), scoping them to the component.

questions.question: Which keyword allows composing styles from another class within the same CSS Module?
questions.explanation: The `composes` keyword lets a class inherit styles from another class within the same module: .btn { composes: base; background: blue; }

questions.question: How do you compose styles from a class in a different CSS Module file?
questions.explanation: The `composes ... from` syntax allows importing styles from another module: .btn { composes: base from './shared.module.css'; }

questions.question: How do you define a global style within a CSS Module?
questions.explanation: The :global() pseudo-selector escapes local scoping, targeting the class name as-is without hashing.

questions.question: What is the recommended naming convention for class names in CSS Modules?
questions.explanation: camelCase is recommended because it allows dot notation access in JavaScript (styles.myClass vs styles['my-class']).

questions.question: What happens when you use multiple classes with CSS Modules?
options: CSS Modules concatenates hashed class names
You must use a helper library
questions.explanation: CSS Modules returns hashed class names that you combine: className={`${styles.class1} ${styles.class2}`} or use libraries like classnames.

questions.question: Can CSS Modules work with CSS preprocessors like SCSS?
options: No, CSS Modules only works with plain CSS
Yes, you can use .module.scss files for SCSS Modules
questions.explanation: CSS Modules work with preprocessors. .module.scss files combine SCSS syntax with CSS Module scoping (SCSS Modules).

questions.question: What is the main advantage of CSS Modules over CSS-in-JS solutions?
options: CSS Modules are faster at runtime
CSS Modules have zero runtime overhead (all processing at build time)
CSS Modules support more CSS features
CSS Modules are easier to debug
questions.explanation: CSS Modules process all scoping at build time with no runtime cost, while CSS-in-JS solutions often have runtime overhead.

questions.question: How does Webpack support CSS Modules?
options: Via css-loader with modules: true option
Webpack does not support CSS Modules
questions.explanation: Webpack's css-loader supports CSS Modules when configured with modules: true, processing .module.css files with local scoping.

questions.question: What is a common way to use CSS Modules with TypeScript for type safety?
options: Use a .d.ts declaration file or generated .module.css.d.ts files
questions.explanation: TypeScript needs declaration files (.d.ts) for CSS Modules. Tools generate .module.css.d.ts files or you use a global declaration.

questions.question: What does `composes: className` do if `className` uses `:global()`?
options: It composes the global class into the local module
questions.explanation: composes respects :global() — if className is marked as global, its styles are still composed into the local module correctly.

questions.question: How do CSS Modules handle animations with @keyframes?
options: Keyframe names are also locally scoped and hashed
Keyframes are converted to inline styles
questions.explanation: CSS Modules also scope @keyframe names locally with hashing, preventing animation name collisions between components.

questions.question: What is the correct way to conditionally apply CSS Module classes in React?
options: Use a classnames library or template literals
All of the above are valid approaches
questions.explanation: CSS Modules combine with standard React patterns: ternaries, &&, classnames library, or template literals for conditional classes.

questions.question: In production mode, what do CSS Modules class names typically look like?
questions.explanation: In production, CSS Modules use short, minified hashes for smaller CSS output. Development mode uses more readable names like Component_class_hash.

questions.question: What does `:local(.className)` do in a CSS Module?
questions.explanation: :local() is the explicit way to scope a class locally (though all classes are local by default in CSS Modules).

questions.question: Which build tools natively support CSS Modules?
options: Webpack, Vite, Next.js, Parcel, and others
None — you need a custom plugin
questions.explanation: CSS Modules are supported by most modern bundlers: webpack (via css-loader), Vite (built-in), Next.js (built-in), Parcel, and more.
